# Transcrição: 05-Zig vs Zag- Dare to Be Different

- **Fonte:** 05-Zig vs Zag- Dare to Be Different
- **Duração:** 06:01
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

A few years ago, Alicia and I went to a wedding and when I arrived I realized I was the only person, well the only man at the wedding who wasn't wearing a tie. In fact I was the only man at the wedding not wearing a button down shirt. I had a black t-shirt, a sports jacket, and a pair of slacks on and sneakers. I stood out like a sore thumb. I didn't care and Alicia didn't care but about halfway through the wedding, slowly at first and then with greater frequency guys started coming up to me and asking me essentially how did you get away with that? How did you get away with a t-shirt and slacks and sneakers? My answer was admittedly not helpful. I told them I do what I want to do. I told them I have a wife who doesn't care what I look like and wants me to be myself. Those were not helpful answers. But what I understood was this, that no one really cared a year later that I wasn't wearing a tie at that wedding. The only people who remembered were those guys who came up to me. Those guys who came up to me and wanted to know how I had managed to do the thing that they wanted to do but couldn't do. Well, the only reason they couldn't dress the way they wanted to dress was the problem that I experienced in corporate America all the time. They felt like they had to be like everyone else. They felt like there was a set of norms and expectations and that if they didn't follow those norms and expectations they would be punished for it. When it turns out no one punished me for not wearing a tie at that wedding. The only thing that happened was a bunch of people recognized me for what I was willing to do and admired me for it. I tell you this story because when I go into businesses, when I go into corporations and we start doing storytelling, everyone tells me that they're excited about telling better stories. That the marketing and the advertising and the sales teams, they're thrilled with the idea of taking on this new challenge. And in the beginning they really are. We're excited about telling stories and we begin engaging in the process in a meaningful and authentic way. But quite often what happens is after we've crafted a really unique story, an authentic story that has vulnerability and craft, other people get involved. And slowly those entertaining, meaningful, connective stories, they get shaved down. And eventually they become what most of corporate America is, which is round, white, and flavorless. Something that is utterly forgettable. It's astounding to me, but it is true, that many of the people who I work with, people

## [03:01 - 06:01]

who have to stand on stages and deliver keynotes, people who have to deliver marketing pitches, most of them want to be forgotten. Or at least that's the way they speak. They want to do what everyone else is doing. And when you're doing what everyone else is doing, you will be forgettable. Storytelling asks you to zig when everyone else is zagging. It asks you to do something that other people are not doing. It asks you to be daring enough and brave enough to stand out when everyone else wants to stand together. For years Steve Jobs would take the stage wearing blue jeans and a black turtleneck. He didn't look like anyone else who was taking stages and delivering talks in those days. He did his own thing and nobody cared. He was producing amazing products and the way he looked was utterly irrelevant to him. Now we remember it. We remember Steve Jobs for lots of reasons, but if you close your eyes and you imagine Steve Jobs, you will see him in a black turtleneck and a pair of blue jeans. He's the only person probably who you imagine in a black turtleneck in blue jeans. If I asked you to envision any other executive standing on a stage today presenting and I asked you to close your eyes, they're probably wearing a suit and a tie. But not Steve Jobs. Steve Jobs for his entire life understood. Zig while everyone else is zagging. Do the thing that is different than what other people are doing. While other people are adding buttons to their phones, we're going to take away all the buttons. Steve Jobs was successful because he dared to be different at all times. I'm asking you to do the same thing. As we move forward in this course, I'm asking you to try to do things that other people are not doing. I'm asking you to see the potential in being memorable. I'm asking you to be daring enough to try new things, things that other people are not daring enough to try. I want you to go to a wedding and a t-shirt and a pair of slacks and sneakers and be remembered for it. Remembered in a positive way. I'm going to ask you to do some things that will make you uncomfortable. I'm going to ask you to do some things that might be frightening to you. It's the only way you'll be remembered. It's the only way you're going to make a difference. It's the only way that the story you're telling will be remembered by the people who are listening to you. I'm asking you to zig while everyone else is zagging and to trust me, it'll make all the difference in the world.
